result:
The first time I realized that words could be places, I was eight years old and hiding inside the broom closet at my grandmother’s house.  The bulb had burned out years earlier, so the darkness was absolute, a textile black you could gather in your hands like velvet.  I had taken a comic book with me, not to read—there was no light—but to press against my face and inhale the newsprint smell, a dry, flaked scent of wagon wheels and distant thunder.  Somewhere between the ink and the paper and the breathing, the closet stopped being a closet.  It dilated into a prairie the color of toast, where men on horses spoke in balloons I could not see but could feel bumping softly against my cheeks like soap bubbles.  When my mother opened the door forty minutes later, the light that tumbled in felt rude, like someone yelling during a lullaby.  I tried to explain where I’d been, but the only language I had was the sentence “I was inside the story,” which sounded like lying, so I learned to keep frontiers to myself.

Years later, long after I had supposedly grown up, I discovered that the brain keeps a map of the places it has inhabited, and that the map is editable. Neuroscientists call this “plasticity,” but I prefer the older, less clinical word: dreaming.  Dreaming is revisionist geography.  You can fold a city block until it touches a summer from decades ago, or swap the scent of lilacs for the sound of a typewriter bell, and the result still feels true, the way a poem feels truer than a balance sheet.  I began to conduct experiments: sitting in laundromats with my eyes closed, I would transplant the river from my hometown directly under the fluorescent lights, so that the socks and jeans tumbled like manatees in brown water.  No one noticed.  People continued feeding quarters into the machines, resigned to the miracle of cloth becoming clean without ever witnessing the miracles underneath.

Sometimes I think we are all amnesiacs surfacing from an immense dream, carrying only the fragments that fit into a pocket or a purse.  A subway turnstile beeps, and for a moment you remember a sea-shanty your choir never sang.  A woman passes wearing the same perfume your aunt wore the year the plum tree died, and suddenly you are back on that lawn again, holding a bucket of pits like tiny skulls.  The world is always pitching these intersecting invitations, but we have appointments to keep, so we seal the visions shut with the glue of obligation.  We call this adulthood, and we are proud of it the way a cast is proud of the bone it immobilizes.

Yet the invitations keep arriving.  They arrive whether or not we RSVP.  One spring I was riding the last commuter train out of Chicago when the carriage lights flickered and went out.  Instead of the expected groan from passengers, a hush settled that felt almost reverent.  In the darkness the train became a whale gliding through a suburb of plankton.  The red signal lights outside floated past like bioluminescent shrimp.  A stranger next to me whispered, “We’re underwater,” and nobody contradicted him.  For thirteen minutes we rode inside a collective metaphor, strangers holding hands without moving.  When the lights returned, we blinked like cavefish and reopened our phones as if nothing embarrassing had happened.  But something had happened: a treaty had been signed in the dark, and though no one spoke of it, the carriage felt lighter, as though we had secretly agreed to carry a portion of one another’s sadness in rotation, the way stones in a river share the weight of the current.

I have come to believe that reality is a thrift store run by an absentminded god.  Nothing is entirely new; everything is secondhand, scarred, half-priced.  We wander the aisles wearing name tags that mispronounce us, trying on professions, relationships, ideologies like mismatched coats.  Occasionally we find a garment that fits so precisely it seems to have been waiting inside our skin for years.  When that happens, we are tempted to call it destiny, but it is more likely a lucky alignment of rips and patches.  The coat doesn’t make us who we are; it reveals who we have always been between disasters.

The problem with revelation is that it refuses to be commodified.  You can’t buy a pound of epiphany or stream it on demand.  It barges in disguised as inconvenience: a delayed flight, a broken vending machine, a urinary tract infection at thirty thousand feet.  The moment is rarely photogenic.  Try posting a picture of yourself crying in an airport restroom, hashtag #blessed.  The algorithm will bury you faster than shame.  Yet those unlovely junctions are where the rewriting happens.  You exit the stall, wash your face with pink slime soap that smells like kindergarten, and suddenly the decade you spent being furious at your father is a house you can no longer enter, not because it’s gone but because the door has become a mirror, and the person on the other side is too busy holding a dripping heart to hold a grudge.

There is, I swear, a physics of mercy.  It operates like dark matter: invisible, inferred only by the way stories bend around it.  I have seen it in hospitals, in courtrooms, in the middle of highways where a dog has been hit and strangers form a silent cathedral of bodies, directing traffic around the small broken body until the screaming owner arrives.  I have felt it in bookstores when a sentence reaches across centuries and taps my sternum, a Morse code that says, “You were never alone in the broom closet.”  The universe may expand until even atoms rip, but mercy is the counterforce, the secret elasticity that lets us stretch without shattering.  It is the reason we can survive being the protagonists of our own epics and the disposable extras in someone else’s war movie simultaneously.

People ask how to become a writer, as though there were a credential for noticing.  The truth is simpler and less marketable: you become a writer the day you realize that you are the only custodian of your immaterial cartography.  No one else will archive the smell of overheated Nintendo cartridges or the exact squeak your sneakers made on the upstairs neighbor’s sun-warped linoleum.  These details are the beads on the abacus of your interior life; slide them however you need to count the days until you forgive yourself.  Then write it down, not because the world needs another memoir, but because the act of writing is a second closet, a place where the bulb can choose to stay off, and the horses can choose to speak in your own accent, and the balloons can lift the roof until it becomes the sky you lost when you were eight and the world yelled during the lullaby.

That is the entire conspiracy: the world is made of words, and the words are made of worlds, and neither is entirely safe, and both are worth entering.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
